options.rationale: HSP requires palpable purpura on dependent areas plus arthritis/abdominal pain/renal involvement; absent here. Would be correct with palpable purpura and IgA features (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Lyme arthritis from Borrelia burgdorferi infection acquired locally in the remote Australian setting despite the absence of erythema migrans rash or any relevant overseas travel
options.rationale: Indigenous Lyme disease is not established in Australia; no relevant travel history, migratory polyarthritis plus carditis plus positive strep fits ARF. Would never be correct in Australia (Source: NHMRC Lyme statement).

explanation.summary: Acute rheumatic fever is diagnosed by the Australian modified Jones criteria, with a lower threshold for diagnosis in high-risk populations (Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander, Maori and Pacific peoples). Major manifestations are carditis (subclinical detectable on echo), arthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum and subcutaneous nodules; minors include fever, raised ESR / CRP, monoarthralgia, prolonged PR. Evidence of preceding GAS (ASOT, anti-DNase B, throat swab) is required, per RHDAustralia 2020 guideline.
key_points: High-risk: 2 major OR 1 major + 2 minor + GAS evidence
Standard-risk: same plus more stringent arthritis definitions
Treat acute episode with bed rest, NSAIDs (after diagnosis), eradicate GAS
Secondary prophylaxis with IM benzathine benzylpenicillin every 21-28 days until at least age 21 and 10 years from last episode
explanation.pearls: Even subclinical mitral regurgitation on echocardiogram now counts as carditis under the 2015 / 2020 Australian modified Jones criteria; always obtain echo when ARF is suspected (Source: RHDAustralia 2020).

subtopic_detail: Post-streptococcal reactive arthritis versus acute rheumatic fever

stem: A 14-year-old boy presents to his GP with recurrent severe right-sided epistaxis over 4 months. Each episode lasts over 20 minutes and on two occasions has required ED attendance for anterior packing. He also reports 3 months of progressive right-sided nasal obstruction and intermittent right cheek fullness. There is no history of trauma, no significant nose picking and no family history of bleeding disorders. He is on no medication. He is otherwise well and growing along the 50th centile. Anterior rhinoscopy reveals a pink-grey mass filling the posterior right nasal cavity that bled briskly on attempted swab. Full blood count and coagulation screen are normal.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 84 bpm, RR 16 /min, BP 116/72 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Right posterior nasal pink-grey mass, brisk bleeding on touch, right-sided nasal obstruction, no cervical lymphadenopathy, no facial nerve weakness

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure as benign anterior Little area epistaxis and prescribe topical Naseptin twice daily for 2 weeks with first-aid advice
options.rationale: Severe recurrent unilateral epistaxis with nasal obstruction and visible posterior mass must not be treated as benign anterior bleeding. Would be correct only in true anterior Little area bleeding (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Attempt bedside biopsy of the visible posterior mass with cup forceps under topical lignocaine to confirm histology before imaging or specialist referral
options.rationale: JNA is exceptionally vascular and clinic biopsy can cause life-threatening haemorrhage; imaging precedes biopsy and biopsy done under GA at theatre. Would never be correct (Source: ENT Society Australia).

options.text: Refer urgently to ENT and arrange contrast-enhanced MRI or CT of the nasopharynx without biopsy in clinic
options.rationale: Recurrent severe unilateral epistaxis with progressive nasal obstruction and a posterior nasal mass in an adolescent male is the classic JNA triad. Urgent ENT referral with contrast cross-sectional imaging is required; biopsy is performed in theatre after imaging and angiographic assessment if needed, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis and BMJ Best Practice.

options.text: Apply silver nitrate cautery to both sides of the nasal septum at Little area today plus topical antibiotic ointment and GP review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Bilateral simultaneous septal cautery risks perforation and does not address posterior nasopharyngeal mass. Would never be correct (Source: RCH CPG).

options.text: Commence oral tranexamic acid 1 g three times daily for 6 weeks as definitive antifibrinolytic therapy
options.rationale: TXA is an adjunct only; does not substitute for ENT assessment and imaging of suspected vascular tumour. Would never be correct (Source: ENT Society Australia).

explanation.summary: Most paediatric epistaxis is anterior and benign, but red flags require ENT referral. The classic triad of juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma is recurrent severe unilateral epistaxis, progressive nasal obstruction and a posterior nasal mass in an adolescent male; clinic biopsy is contraindicated and management requires contrast imaging and specialist surgical (often pre-embolised) resection, per RCH CPG: Epistaxis.
key_points: JNA almost exclusively occurs in adolescent males
Other red flags: bleeding from multiple sites, family history of bleeding disorder, easy bruising, bleeding after dental work
Posterior epistaxis is more common in adults / hypertensive bleeders
Investigations: FBC, coagulation, group and hold; contrast imaging for suspected mass
explanation.pearls: Adolescent boy with severe unilateral nose bleeds plus blocked nose equals JNA until proven otherwise (Source: BMJ Best Practice - JNA).

subtopic_detail: Adolescent epistaxis - red flags for juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma
